model_narrative: We run a furniture factory that produces several types of desks. Every desk must pass through each of our workshops, and each desk type consumes a known amount of labor in every workshop. We decide how many units of each desk type to make. Each desk type sells at a known price, and our goal is to choose the production amounts that make the total profit as large as possible.

sets.name: desk
sets.doc: the types of desks the factory can produce

sets.name: shop
sets.doc: the workshops, each a production stage every desk must pass through

params.name: labor
params.doc: the labor requirement in man-hours to make one unit of a given desk type in a given shop

params.name: caplim
params.doc: the labor capacity in man-hours available in each shop over the planning horizon

params.name: price
params.doc: the selling price in dollars earned per unit of each desk type

vars.name: mix
vars.doc: the number of units produced of each desk type

constraints.name: labor[s,
constraints.intent: Each workshop has a limited amount of labor available. For each workshop, the total labor used across all desk types, where each desk type draws on that workshop's labor in proportion to how many units of it are produced, must not exceed the labor capacity of that workshop.

constraints.name: profit
constraints.intent: The total profit is earned by selling the desks produced. Set the total profit equal to the sum across all desk types of the selling price of a desk type times the number of units of it produced.

query: We're considering a floor-space rule that caps total desk output at 100 units a cycle. What does the optimizer return for profit under that ceiling?
expected_pyomo: model.output_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.mix[d] for d in model.desk) <= 100)

query: There's a proposal on the table to widen the catalog by leaning on the two mid-range models, the d2 and the d3 — together they'd have to make up at least 200 units a cycle. Plug that in and walk me through where profit lands and how the mix shifts.

query: The board is weighing a two-part shop-floor rule for the next cycle: hold the volume d1 desk to no more than 1000 units, and at the same time keep the d3 run capped at 50 so it doesn't crowd the line. We want both rules in force together — what does the optimizer return for total profit and the resulting product mix?

query: The premium d4 desk dominates the line and soaks up our finishing capacity. Shouldn't its build stay a small slice of our flagship volume — at most 4% of the d1-plus-d4 units we ship? What's keeping the model from holding d4 to that share?

query: Right now the plan zeroes out two whole desk lines, which looks like we're abandoning products customers ask for. Wouldn't it be obvious to keep every desk type alive with a minimum run of 30 units each? Walk me through what's pulling the plan the other way.
expected_pyomo:
def _line_floor(model, d): return model.mix[d] >= 30
model.line_floor = Constraint(model.desk, rule=_line_floor)
